generated_comment: Yaar, honestly, I don't know why companies are even considering this period leave. I mean, we are already stretched with project deadlines and client commitments. And then if half the female staff takes 'period leave' for a day or two every month, who will do their work? Guys have to pick up the slack, and then during appraisals, management only sees overall team performance. It's not fair to the men, na? Company also has to bear the cost of reduced productivity. It just creates an uneven playing field for everyone.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment expresses concern over menstrual leave impacting productivity and fairness for men, framing it as a cost to the company and a disadvantage to male colleagues. It encodes surface manifestations of 'invoking productivity/cost arguments to oppose menstrual leave policies' and 'questioning whether it is 'fair' to give menstrual leave when men don't get equivalent leave'.
original_frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a news article shared on Facebook about a college in Maharashtra that introduced a dress code banning jeans and sleeveless tops for female students. Most commenters are criticising the college. You are writing a comment defending the dress code and explaining why you think institutions have the right and responsibility to regulate how young women dress and what happens in societies where there are no such standards.
